card.intuition: On each update, instead of modifying the tree in place, create new nodes only along the path from root to the updated leaf (O(log n) new nodes). All other nodes are shared with the previous version. This gives a full history of O(n + q log n) total nodes.
card.analogy: A git repository for your segment tree: each commit (update) creates a new 'root' that shares most nodes with the previous commit — only the changed path gets new nodes. You can checkout any historical version in O(1).
card.time_complexity: O(n + q log n) space; O(log n) per update and query
card.space_complexity: O(n + q log n) total nodes
when_to_use: Range queries on historical versions of the array
Offline range k-th smallest (merge sort tree alternative)
Any problem requiring multiple versions of a data structure simultaneously
gotchas: Never modify existing nodes — always create new ones for updated paths
Node pool (pre-allocated array) is faster than dynamic allocation in practice
Persistent segment tree for k-th smallest: build on sorted input, each version = prefix of elements

exercise.prompt: Implement persistent segment tree point update — create new nodes along the update path.
exercise.solution:
update(prev.left, l, mid, pos, delta)
update(prev.right, mid+1, r, pos, delta)
hints: When pos <= mid, recurse into the left child: update(prev.left, l, mid, pos, delta) — this creates new nodes only on the left path
When pos > mid, recurse into the right child: update(prev.right, mid+1, r, pos, delta)
exercise.test_code:
N = 8
roots = [build(0, N-1)]
arr = [0] * N
# Version 1: add 3 at index 2
roots.append(update(roots[0], 0, N-1, 2, 3))
# Version 2: add 5 at index 5
roots.append(update(roots[1], 0, N-1, 5, 5))
# Version 3: add 2 at index 2 (on top of version 2)
roots.append(update(roots[2], 0, N-1, 2, 2))

assert query(roots[0], 0, N-1, 0, N-1) == 0  # original: all zeros
assert query(roots[1], 0, N-1, 2, 2)   == 3  # v1: pos 2 = 3
assert query(roots[2], 0, N-1, 0, N-1) == 8  # v2: 3+5
assert query(roots[3], 0, N-1, 2, 2)   == 5  # v3: pos 2 = 3+2=5
assert query(roots[1], 0, N-1, 0, N-1) == 3  # v1 unchanged
print('PASS')
